---
title: "Project Overview"
---

## Introduction/Problem Statement

Diabetes is a growing health concern worldwide, and Singapore is no exception. As one of the most developed nations in Southeast Asia, Singapore has seen a sharp rise in diabetes cases in recent years, posing significant challenges to public health and the healthcare system. According to the Singapore Ministry of Health, approximately 1 in 9 adults in Singapore has diabetes, with the majority of these cases being Type 2 diabetes. This chronic disease is linked to a range of serious long-term complications, including cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, and visual impairment, which not only impact the quality of life for individuals but also contribute to rising healthcare costs and strain the healthcare system. In response to this, the Ministry of Health (MOH) launched the "War on Diabetes" initiative in 2016 to combat the rise in diabetic cases, targeting early prevention, screening and management through policy, education and healthier lifestyle choices.

Despite the ongoing interventions, over the past 3 years, the number of diabetes diagnoses in Singapore has continued to rise, due to multiple contributing factors, such as aging population, urbanization, socioeconomic conditions, demographic differences and lifestyle factors - poor diet and lack of physical activity. A 2020 report by the Ministry of Health revealed that more than 500,000 Singaporeans aged 18 to 69 were living with diabetes, and the country faces an increasing trend in the incidence of diabetes-related complications, such as amputations and dialysis treatments. The significant burden placed on the healthcare system, coupled with the economic costs of managing diabetes, makes it crucial to gain a deeper understanding of how this disease affects various populations in Singapore.

Existing reports and health statistics are often presented in aggregated formats, making it difficult for pattern identification across demographic groups, time periods, or even lifestyle risk factors. This creates an information gap that hinders policy makers and healthcare professionals from easily identifying relationships between contributing factors and translating these insights into targeted and effective interventions.

To address these challenges, understanding the patterns, risk factors, and behaviors that contribute to the rise in diabetes cases is essential for designing targeted interventions, improving patient care, and managing healthcare costs more effectively. Gaining deeper insights into these factors can aid in the development of targeted interventions, improved patient care strategies, and enable more effective management of healthcare resources. This research aims to shed light on these trends through interactive dashboards that uncover patterns, trends, and potential correlations. The study seeks to enhance understanding of diabetes risk factors in Singapore and support better understanding and decision making regarding diabetes early intervention and prevention.

## Motivation

The motivation behind this project stems from the need to better understand the factors contributing to the increasing prevalence of diabetes in Singapore. Diabetes is a complex chronic condition influenced by multiple interacting variables, including age, gender, physical activity, diet, obesity, and socioeconomic conditions. However, traditional statistical reports often present these variables separately, making it difficult to explore relationships between them.

With the knowledge that diabetes has profound social, economic, and health impacts, there is a strong need for analytical approaches that can provide clever insights into these contributing factors. Visual analytics offer a powerful way to address this challenge by transforming complex datasets into visual representations that allow for uncovering critical insights and pattern discovery that can support public health strategies and empower individuals to make healthier lifestyle choices.

Despite ongoing public health initiatives, such as the National Steps Challenge and Diabetes Prevention Program, many people in Singapore still have limited awareness of how lifestyle, socioeconomic factors, and demographics intersect to collectively influence diabetes risk.

This project intends to address this gap by making sense of diabetes-related data from recent years to uncover trends in disease prevalence, associated risk factors, and demographic patterns. Through development of visualizations to present findings, will uncover meaningful insights that can help make complex health information more accessible and understandable to the public, and allow healthcare providers and policymakers to make more informed decisions.

By analyzing diabetes data from the past three years, including trends in disease incidence, risk factors, and geographic distribution, the project aims to identify key patterns that could inform prevention strategies, early detection programs, and healthcare interventions. The goal is to use these findings to contribute to ongoing discussions about how to reduce the prevalence of diabetes in Singapore and improve the quality of life for those affected by the disease.

In summary, this project leverages data-driven insights to promote a better understanding of diabetes in Singapore and assist in the development of evidence-based policies that will reduce the burden of diabetes on individuals and the healthcare system.

## Related Works

### Study 1: Pre-Diabetes Interventions and Continued Tracking to Ease-out Diabetes (Pre-DICTED)

**Objective:** To study the effectiveness of lifestyle intervention with addition of anti-diabetic medication such as metformin, when required, in stopping or reversing the progression from pre-diabetes to type 2 diabetes (HealthXchange, 2017).

**Key Findings:**

- Over 3 years, 34.8% of the intervention participants developed type 2 diabetes compared to 47.3% in the control group - an absolute risk reduction of 12.5 percentage points (Bee et al., 2025).
- Stepped-care intervention - group lifestyle changes with financial incentives and metformin added for 26% of the participants at persistent high risk - reduced type 2 diabetes risk by 26% (Bee et al., 2025).
- The study provided strong evidence that early intervention could significantly prevent diabetes in high-risk individuals.

**References:**

Bee, Y. M., Awasthi, N., Gandhi, M., Lam, A. Y. R., Julianty, S., Tan, G. C. S., Ho, E. T. L., Goh, S.-Y., Tan, G. S. W., Shum, E. J. W., Lee, Y. Q., Chong, M. F. F., Jafar, T. H., van Dam, R. M., Teoh, Y. L., Thumboo, J., & Finkelstein, E. A. (2025). Effectiveness of an Incentives-Enhanced Stepped Care Intervention Program in Diabetes Prevention in a Multiethnic Asian Prediabetes Cohort: Results From the Pre-DICTED Randomized Controlled Trial. *Diabetes Care, 48*(11), 1951–1959. <https://doi.org/10.2337/dc25-1555>

HealthXchange. (2017, October 19). Pre-diabetes FAQs. <https://www.healthxchange.sg/how-to-manage/diabetes-management/pre-diabetes-faqs>

---

### Study 2: Action to Control Cardiovascular Risk in Diabetes (ACCORD)

**Objective:** The ACCORD trial assessed the effects of tight glucose control on cardiovascular events and overall mortality in type 2 diabetes patients at high risk for heart disease.

**Key Findings:**

- Intensive glucose control (targeting A1c below 6%) did not significantly reduce major cardiovascular events.
- It was associated with an increased risk of mortality, leading to the termination of this arm of the trial.
- The findings challenged the benefits of very tight glucose control in high-risk cardiovascular patients.

**References:**

Action to Control Cardiovascular Risk in Diabetes (ACCORD) Study Group, Effects of intensive glucose lowering in type 2 diabetes. *N Engl J Med.* 2008. <https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa0802743>

---

### Current Limitations of the 2 Studies

**Limited Exploration of Multiple Risk Factors Together**

Many landmark diabetes studies focus on specific clinical variables, such as blood glucose control or medication effectiveness. While these studies provide valuable medical insights, they often examine a limited set of variables within controlled environments. This approach may not fully capture the complex interactions between lifestyle, demographic, and physiological factors in real-world settings.

*Our Approach:* To address this gap, our project will analyze datasets containing multiple variables, such as BMI, glucose levels, and lifestyle indicators. By leveraging data visualization tools such as Tableau, we can simultaneously explore the relationships between these factors and uncover patterns that may not be easily observed through traditional clinical analysis.

**Limited Focus on Public Awareness and Preventive Insights**

Many studies concentrate on clinical outcomes and treatment strategies, but they may place less emphasis on presenting findings in ways that promote public awareness and preventive action.

*Our Approach:* Our analysis aims to translate data findings into insights that can help raise awareness about diabetes-related patterns and encourage informed discussions around prevention and early detection. By presenting data in a clear and engaging format, the project can help highlight important health trends that may support better decision-making within society.

## Possible Datasets

- [Mortality Rates Attributed To Suicide, Cardiovascular Disease, Cancer, Diabetes Or Chronic Respiratory Disease, Annual](https://data.gov.sg/datasets?q=&query=diabetes&groups=&organization=&resultId=d_12f48e794a60ef400d9193d2177a9d37)
- [Prevalence Of Overweight, Obesity, Daily Smoking, Hypertension, Diabetes Mellitus, Hyperlipidaemia, Sufficient Total Physical Activity And Binge Drinking Among Residents Aged 18-74 Years](https://data.gov.sg/datasets?q=&groups=&organization=&query=diabetes&resultId=d_711586488b23182801c94bd7b6807833)

**Variables Required:**

- Patient ID: Unique identifier for each patient
- Age: Age of the patient
- Sex: Gender of the patient (Male/Female)
- Diabetes: Diabetic Patients
- Cholesterol: Cholesterol levels of Patients
- Obesity Rates: BMI of Patients
- Physical Activity Levels: Frequency in Exercising
- Blood Pressure: Blood pressure of the patient (systolic/diastolic)
- Smoking: Smoking
- Food & Drink Consumption: Type of Food Intake (Carbs, Protein), Sugar/Calories Count

## Methodology

This project will primarily use Tableau as the main tool for data analysis and visualization. The process will involve gathering relevant diabetes-related datasets, followed by cleaning and preprocessing the data to ensure accuracy and consistency. The prepared data is then used to build interactive visualizations and dashboards in Tableau, enabling visual analytics that uncover trends, patterns, and insights related to diabetes. The steps involved are as follows:

**Step 1: Data Collection**

- **Diabetes Prevalence Data:** The first step will involve collecting relevant datasets from sources like [data.gov.sg](https://www.data.gov.sg) and [singstat.gov.sg](https://www.singstat.gov.sg). The dataset will include information on diabetes prevalence, complications, and healthcare usage.
- **Socioeconomic and Behavioral Data:** We will also gather data on lifestyle factors, such as smoking habits, exercise habits, and socioeconomic status, from [data.gov.sg](https://www.data.gov.sg) and [singstat.gov.sg](https://www.singstat.gov.sg).

**Step 2: Data Preparation in Tableau**

- **Data Import:** Tableau will be used to connect and import data from various sources, including Excel spreadsheets and CSV files, serving as the foundation for analysis and visualization.
- **Data Cleaning:** We will use Tableau data preparation tools such as filtering, grouping, transforming, and pivoting fields to clean and preprocess the data by removing duplicates, handling missing values, and correcting any inconsistencies, and restructuring the data into more analysis-ready format.
- **Data Transformation:** Using Tableau's built-in features, we will create calculated fields and hierarchies (e.g., age groups, regions, BMI categories, etc.) to structure the data, making the data more meaningful and insightful for analysis.

**Step 3: Data Analysis in Tableau**

- **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA):** Using Tableau's interactive features such as filters, sorting, trend lines, and various chart types, we will explore the dataset to identify patterns, distributions, outliers, and relationships within the diabetes-related data. We will use Tableau's drag-and-drop functionality to conduct an exploratory analysis by creating basic visualizations (e.g., bar charts, line graphs, pie charts) to observe trends such as the rise in diabetes prevalence across different age groups, genders, and regions in Singapore.
- **Trend Analysis:** Using time series analysis in Tableau, we will track the yearly trends of diabetes prevalence over the past three years, identifying any significant increases or fluctuations in the data.
- **Segmentation:** We will analyze the data by segmenting it based on age, ethnicity, geographic region, and socioeconomic factors to uncover insights on which populations are most affected by diabetes.
- **Correlation Analysis:** Tableau's correlation matrix and scatter plot capabilities will allow us to explore relationships between diabetes incidence and factors like physical activity, diet, socioeconomic status, and other behavioral factors.

**Step 4: Data Visualization in Tableau**

- **Interactive Dashboards:** Using Tableau's dashboard feature, we will create interactive visualizations that allow users to explore data in more depth. For example, users could filter data by age group, gender, or region to examine specific trends related to diabetes.
- **Geospatial Analysis:** Tableau will be used to map the geographic distribution of diabetes cases across Singapore, allowing for a visual representation of areas with higher or lower diabetic prevalence.
- **Trend Visualizations:** We will present time-based trends with line charts to show the progression of diabetes prevalence over the past three years, and use heat maps to highlight areas or demographics with the highest rates of diabetes.

**Step 5: Insight Generation and Reporting**

- **Dashboard Sharing:** After developing the interactive dashboards, the findings will be shared on Tableau Public. These visualizations will help communicate key insights in an intuitive way.
- **Insight Interpretation:** The final step will involve interpreting the insights generated by the visualizations and providing actionable recommendations. For example, identifying high-risk areas or populations and suggesting targeted intervention strategies, such as lifestyle campaigns or early screening programs.

## Tools and Resources

**Tableau Desktop**

Primary Tool for Data Analysis and Visualization: All aspects of data preparation, analysis, and visualization will be conducted within Tableau Desktop. It will be used for importing, cleaning, transforming, and analyzing the data, as well as for creating interactive dashboards and visualizations.

**Data Sources:**

- [data.gov.sg](https://www.data.gov.sg)
- [singstat.gov.sg](https://www.singstat.gov.sg)

**Tableau Features:**

- **Tableau Prep:** If additional data cleaning or transformation is needed beyond what Tableau Desktop can handle, Tableau Prep will be used for more advanced data preparation tasks.
- **Tableau Public:** For sharing and distributing the final interactive dashboards.

## Project Timeline

| Week | Objectives | Tasks |
|------|------------|-------|
| 9 | Gather and organise all relevant datasets for analysis, and set up Tableau environment | Download all relevant datasets; Review datasets to ensure consistency; Set up Tableau |
| 10 | Clean and prepare data for analysis in Tableau | Import datasets into Tableau; Clean data using Tableau tools; Create calculated fields, group and filter data |
| 11 | Perform EDA and create initial visualisations to uncover trends | Create basic visualisations; Create line charts for trend analysis; Create dashboards |
| 12 | Conduct more detailed analysis and incorporate more advanced charts to visualise geographic trends | Create geospatial visualisations; Create dashboards |
| 13 | Finalise graphs and prepare final deliverables | Create story; Update project website; Create user guide; Create project poster |
